steps.title: Step 1 — the fetch-decode-execute cycle keeps running
steps.body: The processor works through the fetch-decode-execute cycle continuously: fetch the next instruction, decode it, then execute it — regardless of which process currently owns the CPU.

steps.title: Step 2 — check for a waiting interrupt
steps.body: At the **end of every cycle**, immediately after execute and before fetching the next instruction, the processor checks whether an interrupt flag has been set (e.g. by a device or a timer).

steps.title: Step 3 — save the current context
steps.body: If an interrupt is waiting and interrupts are enabled, the current instruction has already finished, so the processor saves the program counter and register values (the process's context) onto the stack before doing anything else.

steps.title: Step 4 — run the interrupt service routine (ISR)
steps.body: Control transfers to the operating system, which uses an interrupt vector or priority to identify the source, then runs the matching **interrupt service routine (ISR)** — for example reading a keystroke, or marking a finished disk transfer as complete.

steps.title: Step 5 — resume normal execution
steps.body: Once the ISR finishes, the processor restores the saved context and resumes the fetch-decode-execute cycle exactly where it left off, so the interrupted process continues as if nothing had happened.
